ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general wonton class; no exact entry for deep-fried shrimp-and-pork wonton variant. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for shrimp-and-pork deep-fried wonton; A0CKP (wonton, deep fried) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of deep fried shrimp and pork wontons is approximately 6 pieces, weighing around 120 g, often served with a seafood dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Deep Fried Shrimp & Pork Wantons — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 炸虾仁馄饨 Deep Fried Shrimp & Pork Wantons (cr_id G00010050). All per-100g values are taken as reported. Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated at 2.8 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central entry for fried wontons (USDA SR Legacy #21322) as a supplementary reference.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for deep-fried shrimp and pork wontons. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the closest analogous food: deep-fried wheat-flour pastry / dim sum wrappers from the University of Sydney GI Database. The HPB input record reports carbohydrate as 0.0 g/100g, which is inconsistent with the presence of wheat-flour wonton wrappers; this is likely a data artefact reflecting the filling-dominant composition of the HPB sample. Glycemic load per serving is calculated as approximately 0 based on the reported 0.0 g carbohydrate per 100g from the HPB source; however, the true GL is likely low-to-moderate given wrapper content. GL is set to 0.0 reflecting the HPB-reported carbohydrate figure. Consumers should note that wonton wrappers do contribute digestible starch.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit deep fried shrimp and pork wontons due to extremely high sodium content (1050 mg/100g); even a small 120 g serving delivers 1260 mg sodium, representing over half the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg, and the low potassium (47 mg/100g) provides no meaningful counterbalance.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Deep-fried refined wheat wonton wrappers with high fat load impair postprandial glycaemic control and insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid deep fried wontons because the deep-frying process raises the effective glycaemic impact of the refined wheat wrappers, the high fat content (10.5 g/100g) slows gastric emptying and promotes postprandial hyperglycaemia, and the negligible dietary fibre (0 g/100g) provides no glycaemic buffering; steamed wontons with a lower-sodium dipping sauce are a preferable alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1050 mg/100g), saturated fat (~2.8 g/100g), trans fat (0.3 g/100g), and cholesterol (89 mg/100g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid deep fried shrimp and pork wontons due to the combination of very high sodium driving arterial pressure, saturated and trans fats raising LDL cholesterol, and dietary cholesterol at 89 mg/100g; the deep-frying method also generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote endothelial inflammation and arterial stiffness.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates AGEs; pork contributes arachidonic acid (omega-6); trans fat (0.3 g/100g) drives pro-inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep fried wontons as the deep-frying process generates AGEs that worsen joint inflammation, pork filling contributes arachidonic acid amplifying inflammatory cytokines, and trans fat from frying oil further drives pro-inflammatory eicosanoid production; substituting with steamed wontons using a shrimp-only or chicken filling is strongly recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Deep Fried Shrimp and Pork Wontons (炸虾仁馄饨) are a popular Chinese-origin dim sum and snack dish widely consumed in Singapore. Each wonton consists of a thin refined wheat-flour wrapper encasing a savoury filling of minced shrimp (prawns) and pork, which is then deep-fried in hot oil until the wrapper becomes golden, blistered, and crispy. The exterior is crunchy and light, while the interior filling is juicy, savoury, and slightly springy from the shrimp. They are typically served with a seafood dipping sauce or sweet chilli sauce. Per 100 g, the dish provides 171 kcal, 19.1 g protein, 10.5 g fat, and 0.0 g carbohydrate as reported by the Singapore HPB Foodo database — the near-zero carbohydrate figure reflects the filling-dominant composition of the HPB sample and does not negate the presence of wheat starch in the wrappers. Sodium is very high at 1050 mg/100g, making this dish unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or fluid retention. The deep-frying method generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and adds trans fat (approximately 0.3 g/100g), raising concern for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis and cardiovascular disease. Cholesterol is 89 mg/100g. This dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those following a halal diet due to pork content. It contains gluten from the wheat wonton wrappers and crustacean allergens from the shrimp filling. Clinically, this dish is high-concern for hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease due to its sodium, fat, and frying method.
common_user_queries: how many calories in fried wontons

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) are sourced directly from the Singapore HPB Foodo database (cr_id G00010050) with a reported confidence of 0.95. Saturated fat is supplemented from USDA SR Legacy as a secondary approved source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database as no direct measurement exists for this dish.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is reported as 0.0 g/100g by the HPB Foodo database for this specific sample. This is confirmed zero per the HPB source and likely reflects the filling-dominant composition of the analysed sample (high protein from shrimp and pork, minimal wrapper contribution in the measured portion). The wonton wrappers do contain refined wheat starch; consumers should be aware that the true carbohydrate content may be higher depending on wrapper-to-filling ratio. Sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB source. Dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — naturally zero given the refined wheat wrapper and absence of vegetable ingredients in the filling. Saturated_fat_g (2.8 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried wontons as HPB did not report this value; this is an approved secondary source. Trans_fat_g (0.3 g/100g) is carried forward from the input health_suitability caution note; this value is not directly reported in the HPB per_100g block and should be verified against the original HPB source record. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g, and all vitamin fields set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, moisture, and micronutrient data beyond those reported by HPB are not available in the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish, and no sufficiently close USDA SR Legacy match provides a complete fatty acid breakdown for this specific preparation. Glycemic load per serving is set to 0.0 based on the HPB-reported carbohydrate of 0.0 g/100g; the true GL is likely low-to-moderate given wrapper starch content. This dish contains pork and is not halal. Allergens: crustaceans (shrimp), gluten/wheat (wonton wrappers).
